Organize Your GitHub Stars With Tags

If you're a developer, you've probably starred a bunch of projects on GitHub. But once your Stars list grows, finding the one you actually need turns into a pain. So this post is about how to actually organize the projects you've starred.
GitHub added a Stars Lists feature to the Stars page a while back. Just head to your profile, click the Stars tab up top, and you'll see Stars Lists.
Stars Lists is what we're focused on this time. It's basically tag-based categorization.
How do you use it? First, click the Create list button.
You'll see two fields: "Name this list" and "write a description".
Let me show you by creating a Vue tag.
After creating it, you'll get redirected to the tag's page. If you see this screen, congrats — it's created.

Now how do you actually put your starred projects in there? Head back to the Stars page, find the project you want to categorize, click the little arrow (more), and you'll see the Vue option you just made. Check it.
And of course, if you've made multiple tags, you can pick more than one.
Once you tag your starred projects, you can manage them straight from the list at the top of the Stars page.
For people who like to categorize stuff, this is great.
You can also tag projects before you star them, if you prefer.

One more nice thing about this feature: you can use it to organize Private projects, because tagged Private projects don't show up publicly. For example, I added my Private Hexo system to a "test" tag.
So other people will see the tag but not what's inside.
If you've been wondering forever whether you could categorize your stars — like I have — you can finally relax. GitHub supports it.
